description.value: The Saltwyrm will not enter water shallower than 10 feet or thick with coral. The reef is safety.
The Saltwyrm deals double damage to objects and structures, including ships.
The Saltwyrm never leaves the sea.
The Saltwyrm makes one Bite attack and uses Constrict.

Melee Weapon Attack: +10 to hit, reach 10 ft. Hit: 22 (3d10 + 6) piercing damage. A Large or smaller target is grappled (escape DC 16) and, at the start of the wyrm’s next turn, dragged underwater unless freed.

The wyrm coils around a vessel within 10 feet. The ship is grappled, its speed becomes 0, and it takes 22 (4d10) bludgeoning damage at the start of each of the wyrm’s turns. The coil has AC 15 and 45 HP.

The Saltwyrm submerges and surges into a vessel within 60 feet. The vessel takes 33 (6d10) bludgeoning damage, doubled by Siege Monster. Each creature aboard makes a DC 15 Dexterity save, taking 11 (2d10) bludgeoning damage and falling prone on a failure; a failure by 5 or more also throws the creature overboard.
The Saltwyrm dives. It is heavily obscured and has half cover until it next surfaces. It cannot be rooted while submerged.
Legendary Action (costs 2): Melee Weapon Attack: +10 to hit, reach 15 ft. Hit: 17 (2d10 + 6) bludgeoning damage.
Legendary Action (costs 2): The Saltwyrm swims up to its speed without provoking opportunity attacks, resurfacing in a new position.
The Mistling can move through other creatures and objects as if they were difficult terrain. It takes 1d10 force damage if it ends its turn inside an object.
While within 20 feet of a Stillpoint, or of a creature that used an Anchor since the start of its last turn, the Mistling has disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks.
Melee Spell Attack: +4 to hit, reach 5 ft. Hit: 10 (3d6) cold damage, and the target cannot regain hit points until the start of its next turn.
The Hollow has advantage on attacks against any creature at Fading stage 2 or higher.
Melee Weapon Attack: +5 to hit, reach 5 ft. Hit: 9 (2d6 + 2) bludgeoning damage. The target must succeed on a DC 13 Wisdom save or advance one stage on the Fading track.
A character who knows the Hollow’s true name may speak it as an action. The Hollow must succeed on a DC 13 Wisdom save or be incapacitated until the end of its next turn.
The Pale Sentinel is immune to command, heat metal, and hold monster.
The Pale Sentinel makes two Spectral Blade attacks.
Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft. Hit: 13 (2d8 + 4) force damage.
Reaction, once per round: when hit, the Sentinel blurs up to 15 feet through fog to an unoccupied space, imposing disadvantage on the triggering attack.
The Pale Sentinel is immune to command, heat metal, and hold monster.
The Pale Sentinel makes two Spectral Blade attacks.
Melee Weapon Attack: +7 to hit, reach 5 ft. Hit: 13 (2d8 + 4) force damage.
Reaction, once per round: when hit, the Sentinel blurs up to 15 feet through fog to an unoccupied space, imposing disadvantage on the triggering attack.
The Court’s Sword has advantage on saving throws against spells and other magical effects.
If the Court’s Sword fails a saving throw, it can choose to succeed instead.
At 84 hit points or fewer, the Pall thickens. At the start of each of the Sword’s turns, every creature within 20 feet that cannot see clearly through fog makes a DC 13 Fading save.
The Court’s Sword makes three Greatsword of Mist attacks.
Melee Weapon Attack: +9 to hit, reach 10 ft. Hit: 16 (2d10 + 5) force damage.
30-foot cone. Each creature in the area makes a DC 16 Wisdom save, taking 27 (6d8) psychic damage and advancing one Fading stage on a failure, or half damage and no Fading on a success.
Reaction: when a creature within 10 feet misses the Sword with a melee attack, the Sword makes one Greatsword of Mist attack against it.
Legendary Action: the Sword moves up to half its speed without provoking opportunity attacks.
Legendary Action: one Greatsword of Mist attack.
Legendary Action (costs 2): until the start of its next turn, attacks against the Sword have disadvantage as it blurs into fog.
The shark has advantage on melee attack rolls against any creature that does not have all its hit points.
The shark can breathe only underwater.
Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft. Hit: 13 (2d8 + 4) piercing damage.

di.custom: Divine Beast: cannot be defeated or reduced to a combat encounter

description.value: Hauiri is immune to all damage and every condition. Weapons and effects pass through it as through fog. Powerful effects may disperse it for up to 1 round; it reforms nearby, unharmed. There is no combat resolution to this encounter.
While Hauiri is within 120 feet, vision is heavily obscured beyond 15 feet. Creatures ending their turn within 30 feet of it make a DC 13 Fading save. It radiates no malice.
Hauiri pads ahead and looks back. The offered path is always safe and always leads deeper—toward the palace and itself.
Hauiri draws close to a Fading creature and takes a memory; in exchange the Pall parts completely for one minute.
Hauiri opens an echo of the old world. Witnessing it costs a Fading save but reveals a true clue.
One creature answers with a true, specific memory spoken aloud and steps its Fading back by one. Refusal, a lie, or a false name advances Fading by one.

Ranged siege attack: +6 to hit, range 120 ft. Hit: 3d10 piercing damage. On a hit it roots the Saltwyrm, preventing Submerge or Ram, until the wyrm escapes (DC 16) or 25 damage cuts the line. Requires one action to load and one to fire.
At 45 HP the vessel founders: speed is halved and helm checks are made with disadvantage. At 0 HP it sinks over approximately 3 rounds.
Track a six-segment clock. Helm successes and favorable rounds fill it. When it fills, the vessel reaches the coral and the Saltwyrm breaks off.
Helm/run for the reef; repair the hull for 2d8 HP; secure crew or cut coils; call the dive; load or fire the harpoon-thrower.

text.content:
MAUREI, THE ISLE THAT REMEMBERS — Complete Adventure Pack

A one-shot for a 5-person, level 7 party · The Golden Triangle

About this document. This is the complete, self-contained GM pack for the Maurei one-shot, consolidating the adventure, the combat expansion ("Teeth in the Mist"), the beat-by-beat environmental guide, all stat blocks, and every final ruling. It supersedes earlier drafts — in particular: there are no moonlight mechanics anywhere (Hinaroa, the moon, is a timekeeper and navigator's mark only, never a power); memory-based Stillpoints do every job moonlight once did; the palace is called the Pale Court in all player-facing text; the reef encounter uses the Saltwyrm and Hunter Sharks (the Drowned never appear — they are reserved for the main campaign); and the climax is staged at the throne that faces the world, not in any beam of light.

The Golden Triangle is a Polynesian-inspired world that is nearly all ocean. Its people sail between scattered islands; navigation, memory, and the sea are everything. The sun is Arohena, the moon is Hinaroa (a celestial clock and steering-mark — no mystical power), the stars are Fetuariki. Divine Beasts are vast, unkillable powers that reshape islands where they pass. The Shadow Isles are remote mysteries at the chart's edge.

GM-ONLY, never player-facing before the campaign reveals it: the world was once a continent, Arkhand. Its barons made a pact with the Dreamer of the deep; the world drowned, and the barons were reborn as the Divine Beasts. Nothing in this one-shot states this — the island only gestures at it through foreign architecture, unreadable script, and echoes.

You have a heading and a name. Maurei. It sits on the charts like a smudge someone tried to rub out — drawn in three different places by three different hands, ringed each time with the same warning: coral, mist, do not land.

But each of you carries a reason to land anyway. A debt. A grave. A question. A map bought from a dying man. The captain who agreed to bring you this far will take you no closer than the reef-line — and even that, she says, is further than a sensible woman would go.

Ahead, the island is a low green hump wearing a crown of cloud that does not move with the wind. The sea around it has gone strangely quiet.

Built for roughly 3–4 hours of play: a hard approach and landing, a disorienting ascent, and a haunted palace where the island's secret — and the party's own memories — are the true stakes.

Maurei is a piece of Arkhand that never finished drowning.

Before the Great Ocean swallowed the world, there was a continent, and on it the barons of Arkhand, who made their pact with the Dreamer of the deep — drowning the world to be reborn as the Divine Beasts. This island is the high seat of one such baron: Baron Aurel Velmaire, whose court stood on a mountain too proud to sink.

Velmaire took the pact like the others. He became a Beast. But where his peers became leviathans and storms, Velmaire became fog — the soft, total forgetting at the bottom of a dream. And a thing that is made of forgetting cannot remember what it is. Century by century, the Baron has been dissolving into his own mist, until now only a shape remains: a pale fox, curious and sorrowful, walking the halls of a home it can no longer name.

The island "remembers" the old world — the palace stands, the court still dances as an echo — precisely because Hauiri the Mistfox has forgotten to let it go. And everyone who lands begins to forget too, because to stand in the Baron's breath is to be slowly written out of your own story, the way he was written out of his.

The fox is not a guardian and not a predator. It is a drowned man dreaming he is an animal. It will not attack. It will follow, and watch, and ask — without words — the one question it can no longer answer about itself: who are you? The party's danger is not its teeth. It is that the island is a soft place to set your name down and never pick it back up.

The key to every "good" ending is the Baron's name, which survives above water in fragments: the half-erased journal one of the PCs carries (Secret 5), the corroded portrait plate in the Hall of Faces, and the throne-room inscription.

The Pall

The entire island stands inside the Pall — a standing mist that is the Beast's breath and presence made weather. It does three things, always:

  • It blinds. Across most of the island, vision is lightly obscured beyond 30 ft and heavily obscured beyond 60 ft. In "deep Pall" (the Whisperwood interior and the palace), it is heavily obscured beyond 15 ft. Bright light sources carry only half as far and take on a cold, pale cast.
  • It unmoors. The Pall makes distance and direction lie. Maps drawn on the island contradict themselves. Paths are not where you left them (see Mist-Events). Survival checks to navigate are made at disadvantage; a natural sense of "the way back" simply does not work.
  • It forgets. This is the core mechanic. Prolonged exposure erodes memory — see The Pall & Fading. The deeper in, the faster it bites.

The one true mercy: the Stillpoints. In a few places on Maurei the Pall simply thins — not because of any light, but because something there is too remembered to forget: a grave someone still tends in the echo, a threshold worn by a thousand feet, a room so loved it won't fade. A Stillpoint is a breather and a safe-ish island in the mist: a creature that rests there a few minutes may step its Fading back by one (see Anchors). This is also your pacing safety valve: when a table is drowning, give them a Stillpoint.

The shape of the place

Maurei rises in three rough bands, and the adventure climbs through them:

  1. The Reef and the Pale Strand — the killing coral, the black-sand shore, the first breath of mist.
  2. The Whisperwood and Forgotten Slopes — a fog-drowned forest where time runs wrong and the fox first walks beside you.
  3. The Pale Court — the palace of Baron Velmaire, where the truth and the prize both wait.

Read aloud:

The captain's been quiet for an hour, which is how you know she's afraid. Maurei sits low and green ahead, wearing its motionless crown of cloud, and the sea between you and it has gone glassy and wrong — the swell long and slow, like the ocean is breathing in its sleep. Then a shadow the length of the ship slides under the keel, unhurried, and is gone.

The story in the details. The water tells the story before the beast does. The closer you draw, the quieter it gets — gulls peel away toward open sea, fish stop breaking the surface, the wind dies a half-mile out so the sails go slack. The mist-crown never shifts, whatever the wind does elsewhere. Let the captain mutter the lore the players need: we hug the shallows — it won't come where the coral is. The reef should read as refuge before the Saltwyrm ever surfaces.

What they can find:

  • The slick. Oily calm water dotted with pale, dinner-plate scales and snapped lengths of someone else's harpoon-line. A Nature or Survival check: it sheds before it feeds. It is hungry and territorial, not malicious — useful for players who'd rather not kill it.
  • Old warnings. A drift-buoy at the channel mouth hung with an offering — shells, a carved hook, sailcloth painted with a warding eye. A sailor's rite for safe passage; a Tidewarden knows it. Honoring it is pure flavor.
  • The captain's tell. Pressed, she admits she lost a cousin's ship "out past the coral," and will say no more. She is not coming ashore. People who go to Maurei do not come back whole — said without a word of why.

The fight. A Saltwyrm rises to attack. It will not enter the coral, so the goal is to reach the reef — though a bloodthirsty crew may kill it instead. (See Naval Combat and the Saltwyrm stat block.)

Read aloud:

The white water is a wall of noise after the silence, and through it the coral shows its teeth — black and red and bone-pale. Caught in it, canted and barnacled, are the ribs of older ships than yours. And past the reef the mist begins, not rising off the water but simply standing there, waiting, as if it has been expecting you.

The story in the details. The reef is a graveyard of approaches, and the wrecks are a timeline — they sit at different depths of decay, so the players can see for themselves that this has been happening a long time, and is still happening. The mist's edge is a hard line: warm spray on one side, cold still air on the other, and flames gutter low the moment they cross it. The first proof the island does something to people, delivered as weather, not exposition.

What they can find:

  • The recent wreck. A soaked logbook; a chart of the island confident near the shore, dissolving into crossed-out guesses and then blank paper inland; and a journal whose pages look water-ruined but are actually erased — ink faded as if the words forgot themselves. One name survives, scored hard enough to scar the page. Do not explain it. (Ties to Secrets 2 & 5.)
  • The provisioner's cache. A sealed cask of still-good water and hardtack — a welcome resupply, and a quiet horror: packed for a long stay by people who expected to come home.
  • The cairn. On a coral head above the tide, a stack of ballast-stones topped with a child's carved toy boat. No body. No name. The emotional keynote of the whole island, found in the first five minutes ashore.
  • Salvage. A Salvager appraises a few intact trade-goods worth carrying — and feels which wrecks are unstable. One will shift and dump its cargo, and anyone aboard, into the coral if disturbed.

The fight. Run the coral landing as a hazard (see The Reef Landing). If the Saltwyrm drew blood, the reef's own hunters arrive — 3 Hunter Sharks, a fast, low-stakes water fight. Reaching the black-sand shore ends it; they won't beach.

First Fading save (DC 10) as the party clears the gap: the surf drops to glassy stillness, the temperature falls, and the mist closes behind them like a door. Maurei takes its first breath of them.

Read aloud:

The black sand gives way to a forest that is wrong in a way you can't place — then you have it: nothing moves. The pale, half-petrified trees don't sway. No birds, no insects. Your own footsteps come back to you a half-second late. And the fog, which had been at your knees, rises without a sound until the friend ahead of you is a grey shape, then a suggestion, then a wall of white.

The story in the details. The wood is memory made into landscape — a place where time and direction have come loose. Use small wrongnesses: a flower blooms and browns and dies as you look at it; a path of flat stones leads back where it started; foreign carved markers stand at intervals, older than anything should be here. The fogwall closes deliberately, to split the party, and the dead come from the side you can no longer see. Play the separation for dread — each group hears the other's fight but cannot reach it.

Before the wood: the fox's first appearance. On the Pale Strand, far up the slope through a gap in the fog, something white moves. It stops. It watches. Then the fog closes and it is gone. Always at a distance; it flees the moment anyone moves toward it. Establish early: you cannot catch it, and it is not chasing you.

Set-pieces to seed through the wood:

  • The Memory-Spring. A pool of still, silver water. Drinking it heals (a short rest's worth of dice) but costs a memory — a Fading save, and on a fail the drinker forgets what they just drank for. A PC who drinks deeply may glimpse an echo in the water: courtiers, lantern-light, a tall fogged figure on a balcony.
  • The Hunt That Isn't. Horns, hounds, laughter — a lord's hunting party, centuries dead, passing as an echo through the trees. Watching costs a Fading save. Within it, an observant PC sees the lord of the hunt: a man with a fox at his heel — or is the fox at the heel, or leading? (This foreshadows Beat 4, where the echo curdles.)
  • The Tended Grave — a Stillpoint. A clearing where a low stone is clean of moss, the ground before it swept, dried flowers laid as if this morning. No one is there. No one is ever there. A true refuge: the Pall thins, Fading recovery is possible (see Anchors & Stillpoints), and the fox may sit at the treeline and simply watch — the party's first chance to interact rather than flee. This is the heart of Act II; make sure the party finds it.

What they can find:

  • The marker-stones. Pale, squared, carved — foreign to any island stonework the characters know. A scholar can tell they were cut, not natural, and are far older than the trees grown up around them. They lead somewhere, if followed.
  • The half-faces. A dispersed Mistling leaves, for a moment, a clear impression of a living face — frightened, ordinary. These were people. The fog is not summoning monsters; it is keeping the forgotten.
  • The blaze-trail. Knife-marks on trunks where someone tried to mark a path. They lead deeper, then stop — the last cut half-finished, the knife still buried in the bark below a smear of cold sweat.
  • A dropped thing. Snagged on a root: a personal token — a ring, a locket, a comb — that can match a description from one of the PCs' secrets. Holding it is an Anchor against Fading; recognizing it is a gut-punch.

The fight. The fogwall splits the party and 5–6 Mistlings drift in from the blind side.

Read aloud:

Horns. Far off, then not far off. Hounds giving tongue, and under it the easy laughter of people enjoying themselves — a hunting party, alive and merry and centuries gone. Then the laughter stops all at once, the baying turns wrong, and out of the white comes a tall pale figure with a blade of fog and a pack at its heel — and they have caught your scent instead.

The story in the details. This reveals what the island was without a word of lore — that there were lords here once, fine and idle enough to hunt for sport, in a place no one should have been able to live. The echo plays as a pleasant memory for a few seconds before it curdles, so the players feel the tragedy a half-beat before the threat. The huntsman fights with cold courtesy and never speaks; the "hounds" course and flank like real dogs. Fight near a Stillpoint if you can, so the remnants falter where the dead are too well remembered to hold their edge.

What they can find:

  • The quarry. Backtrack the hunt and you find what it was chasing — not an animal, but a trail of dropped effects: a shawl, a shoe, a satchel. The "game" here has not been deer for a very long time.
  • The huntsman's token. The fallen guardian leaves a tarnished horn or signet bearing the same heraldry the players will meet again in the Pale Court — a device with a fox worked into the crest. The fox and this place belong to each other. Let them theorize.
  • The kennel-stone. A flat stone set with iron rings and the ghosts of names scratched beneath each — the hounds' names, the only words in the whole wood a character can almost read. Even the dogs are remembered here, and cannot leave.

The fight. One Pale Sentinel (the huntsman) and 4 Mistlings (the hounds).

Read aloud:

The trees argue with marble and the marble wins, and you are inside before you decide to be: a vast foreign hall, dripping, faintly luminous in the fog, where thin formal music plays from rooms you haven't reached. Two pale figures in old armor stand at a doorway as they have stood for an age — and as you enter, their heads turn together, with the slow attention of things that have waited a very long time for someone to guard against.

The story in the details. The palace is opulence drowned and risen — every surface beaded with water, as if the sea only just left it. The architecture is wrong-foreign: proportions, script, and ornament that match nothing in the Triangle and everything in the marker-stones and the huntsman's crest. Let that consistency do the work; explain none of it. The Sentinels guard specific rooms, which quietly tells the players where the valuable things are. Light misbehaves — your shadow lags, and the standing water reflects the rooms as they were: lit, dry, full of people. Deep Pall throughout; Fading saves accelerate here (DC 15).

What they can find:

  • The Hall of Faces. Portraits of one proud line. The largest, at the end, has had its face dissolved to fog by time — but under close inspection (Investigation DC 15), or when a character speaks the name aloud, the brushwork resolves into a figure that is also a pale fox, the two painted as one. The corroded nameplate is almost legible, and matches the name in the wreck-journal. This is the heart of the puzzle. No further reveal.
  • The defended room. Whatever the Sentinels stand before is worth the standing: a strongroom, a reliquary, a study where a letter sits half-finished in that unreadable hand, the ink still wet, dated to no calendar the players use.
  • The water-mirrors. Pools that reflect the past. A PC who lingers sees the room alive — and, if they look too long, sees themselves among the courtiers, dressed in old finery and perfectly at home. A Fading hazard, and a chilling foreshadow of becoming Lost.
  • The drip-map. A Boatwright or careful explorer notices the whole palace is canted a few degrees and weeping at every seam. It is risen, not built here — and it is very slowly going back down.
  • The Vault / Reliquary. The prize (see Treasure). Taking it is easy. Keeping it clean of the Pall's price is the trick.

The fight. 2 Pale Sentinels guarding a key chamber.

Read aloud:

Ankle-deep water across a floor that was once gold, and turning across it — slow and formal and translucent — a whole court at its dance. Gowns and coats a century out of any fashion you know, faces serene, eyes shut, moving to music that has no players. None of them have looked at you. Not yet. And there is an empty space in the figure of the dance, as though it waits for a partner who hasn't arrived.

The story in the details. The most beautiful and most dangerous room — play wonder first, horror second. The dancers are gentle; they welcome the party, bowing them in, addressing them by the names of the long-dead, offering a hand. Nothing here is hostile until invited: the fight only starts if a PC dances, refuses too rudely, or disrupts the figure, at which point the lesser ones tear loose. The empty place is the hook — someone is missing from this court, and the palace would dearly like to fill the gap with one of the living. Accepting a dead courtier's name is a Fading hazard; a PC who dances risks losing themselves to the figure they're "playing."

What they can find:

  • The place-cards. A banquet table set under dust and brine, each setting named in the foreign hand — except one card, scraped blank, at the seat beside the head of the table. Someone was removed from this court's memory, deliberately.
  • A familiar face. For the grieving-kin player (Secret 2): among the dancers, eyes shut and content, is the person they came to find — Lost, fully Faded, dancing. Reachable only by a true memory spoken aloud. Approaching is safe; waking them is the hard part, and the climax of that PC's one-shot.
  • The orphan instrument. A single real instrument rests on a chair, warm to the touch, recently played. Lift it and the ghost-music falters and the whole court slowly opens its eyes. A perfect "do we dare" object.
  • The cloakroom. A side chamber of pegged cloaks and finery, dry and untouched — and among them, far newer coats and oilskins belonging to recent visitors, hung up neatly as if their owners meant to come back for them. They didn't.

The fight (optional). If provoked, the court turns: 6 Mistlings and 2 Hollows.

Read aloud:

The music is behind you now, and ahead is a pale seat of stone, empty, and curled in the hollow where a body once sat is the fox — small, white, patient, watching you with eyes like low lamps. Between you and it, a figure rises that did not rise for the dancers or the portraits: tall, armored in fog given an edge, lifting a greatsword of mist. The fox does not move to help it. The fox only watches, the way you'd watch weather.

The story in the details. Everything converges here. The throne is the one seat that faces a window looking back over the path the party climbed — the only place in the palace that faces outward, toward the world the fox came from and forgot. The Pall is thinnest there: sat in that seat, a person cannot help but remember there was a life beyond this island. The Sword is the palace's last reflex, not the fox's bodyguard — its indifference to the fight is the point. The room is dressed for a reveal you withhold: the empty chair, the fox-and-figure portrait on the far wall, the blank place-card, the half-name — all of it right there, so the players assemble the truth themselves while you never state it.

What they can find:

  • The throne-side table. A writing-stand holds the last, completed version of the letter from the halls — and beneath it, pressed flat, a child's drawing or a lock of hair kept by someone who is now a fox. The most human object on the island, and the thing the fox is curled around.
  • The name, completed. With the wreck-journal's scarred name, the portrait's nameplate, and the throne's inscription together, the players assemble the whole name — the key to the ending. Speaking it at the seat that faces the world releases the fox: it remembers, for a moment, that it was a person. The music stills, the dancers sink, the palace begins its long slide back under. Who he was stays unspoken — a thread for the main campaign to pull.
  • The fox's parting image. As they pull away through the reef, give them one last picture: the mist-crown thinning, or not — and, for a single breath, a small pale shape at the shoreline, watching them go. Grief, gratitude, or nothing at all: the question they take home.

The fight. The Court's Sword — a true boss. Add 2–3 Mistlings for a strong party. When it falls, the fox merely lifts its head.

Each character tracks Fading, a five-stage spiral. A character makes a Fading save (a Wisdom saving throw) when they: first set foot on the island; rest or linger in deep Pall; come within the fox's Presence; drink the Memory-Spring; witness an echo; are hit by certain remnants; or refuse/fail the fox's question. Base DC 13 (DC 10 on the Strand, DC 15 in the palace; +1 per prior failure that scene). On a failure, advance one stage:

Stage Name What the island takes
1 Haze Disadvantage on checks that rely on memory or familiarity (recalling lore, retracing a path). A small detail slips — a date, a color, a word.
2 Slip Forget one prepared spell, the location of one carried item, or one proper noun (the ship's name, an ally back home). Until recovered, it's simply gone.
3 Drift Forget why you came — your Secret's goal goes blank. Forget one companion's name. Lose one use of a class feature you "can't quite remember how to do."
4 Unmooring Forget a piece of your past — a Bond, your home island, a loved face. You begin to feel the palace is familiar, even welcoming. The fox starts to treat you as one of its own.
5 Lost You forget you ever lived anywhere else. You wander, content, into the mist and become an echo of Maurei — removed from play. Only a powerful Anchor, applied by another in the same scene, can call you back from the brink.

Several remnants can inflict Fading on a hit, so those fights cost more than hit points.

The forgetting is not one-way. A character steps Fading back by one when:

  • A companion speaks aloud a true, specific memory of who that character is ("You're the one who carried me off the Kestrel with two arrows in your leg") — roleplay it; reward specificity.
  • They hold and focus on a treasured personal object they brought from home (have each player name one in advance — it's a built-in anchor and a great character beat).
  • They rest a few minutes at a Stillpoint.
  • They answer the fox's question truthfully (see Ask).
  • They leave the island. Fading below stage 4 clears over a few days at sea; stage 4 may leave a scar; stage 5 does not return.

The Stillpoints. Places where the Pall thins because something there is too remembered to forget: a grave still tended in the echo, a threshold worn by a thousand feet, a room so loved it won't fade. A breather, a chance to step Fading back — and tactical ground: remnants falter on remembered ground (see the Mistling's Anchored Ground trait). The players' own acts of remembering are the weapon.

Roll or choose when the party rests, splits, lingers, or pushes deeper.

d8 Event
1 Fogwall. A wall of mist rises and splits the party in two; each group can hear but not reach the other. It thins in 1d10 minutes — or when they reach a Stillpoint.
2 Tidewrong. The way back has changed: the path, the reef-gap, or the very shoreline is no longer where they left it. Re-navigation required (disadvantage).
3 Timeslip. 1d4 hours vanish between one step and the next, or full day flips to night. An interrupted rest grants no benefit; rations are mysteriously gone.
4 Rememory. An echo of the old world plays out around them (a feast, a hunt, a quarrel on a balcony). Fading save to watch; a true clue to those who do.
5 The Pale Door. A doorway or stair appears in a blank wall or cliff, inviting and warm, leading deeper. It is a real shortcut — toward the fox.
6 Doubling. A PC briefly meets a mist-double of themselves, or another party member is replaced by a silent echo for a few minutes. Unsettling; not (yet) hostile.
7 Stillness. All mist withdraws for one minute and the island holds its breath. The fox is right there, watching, close enough to touch — and gone the instant anyone reaches.
8 The Question. The fox arrives and Asks (see its presence block). Center the scene on one PC.

Design note. A Divine Beast is not a boss fight; it is a condition of the world. Hauiri has no hit points the party can spend, and any table that tries to "win" by damage should hit a wall — gently, clearly, early — and be redirected toward the real game: navigation, memory, and understanding. This is a presence block, not a stat block.

HAUIRI, THE MISTFOX — Divine Presence (cannot be defeated)

  • Made of Mist. Immune to all damage and every condition. Weapons and effects pass through it as through fog. A powerful effect (gust of wind, a high-level radiant burst, daylight) may disperse it for up to 1 round; it reforms nearby, unharmed and unchanged. There is no combat resolution to this encounter and the party cannot acquire one.
  • Presence (the Pall). While Hauiri is within 120 ft, the Pall thickens: vision heavily obscured beyond 15 ft, and creatures that end their turn within 30 ft of it make a Fading save (DC 13). It radiates no malice — only a deep, pulling quiet, like the moment before sleep.
  • Not Hostile. It never initiates harm. Cornered or struck, it simply unmakes and reappears elsewhere. Its disposition is curious and grieving.

Reactions, not attacks. Each scene it shares with the party, it may do one of the following:

  • Lead. It pads ahead and pauses, looking back: an offer of a path. The path is always true (safe), but always leads deeper — toward the palace, toward itself.
  • Take. It draws close to a Fading creature and breathes. The creature loses a memory of the GM's choosing — a prepared spell, an expended feature, a fact, a face — and in exchange the Pall parts completely for one minute. A devil's bargain offered without words.
  • Show. It opens an echo: a scene of the old world plays out around the party (lore, a clue, a glimpse of the Baron-that-was). Witnessing costs a Fading save but gives a true piece of the puzzle.
  • Ask. It sits, fixes one PC with its pale eyes, and the question arrives in the body rather than the ears: who are you? Answer with a true, specific memory of yourself, spoken aloud, and step your Fading back by one. Refuse, lie, or answer with a name that isn't really yours, and advance your Fading by one.

Holding the line in a fight-heavy run: when players itch to swing, the mist provides remnants — they can fight all they like and it never touches the fox. Let Hauiri drift to the edge of a remnant battle and trigger an event (a fogwall, a skipped round, the sudden stillness where it is right there). It shapes the fight; it is never a target in it. The payoff was never a health bar — it is the name.

1. Name it. Recover Baron Velmaire's name (journal + portrait plate + throne inscription, cross-referenced) and speak it at the throne that faces the world. For one moment Hauiri remembers it is a man — gratitude, grief, release. The Pall lifts, the echoes still, the palace finally begins to sink. The party must run for the reef as the island lets go. (The "lay him to rest" ending.)
  2. Trade it. Strike the bargain the fox keeps offering — give it something it wants (a memory, the dance, a name freely set down) in exchange for what you came for and clear passage out. Everyone leaves; not everyone leaves whole. (The "pay the toll" ending.)
  3. Flee it. Take what you grabbed and outrun the forgetting back to the launch before anyone Fades past saving. Maurei keeps its mystery, and its mist, and waits. (The "no one needs to understand a thing to survive it" ending.)

Make sure the table feels, by the end, that the satisfying thing was never going to be a health bar hitting zero. The Sword gives them the fight. The fox gives them the ending.

The vessel:

  • AC 15 · HP 90. At 45 HP it founders: speed halved, helm checks at disadvantage. At 0 HP it sinks — about 3 rounds to reach the launch, the reef, or the water.
  • Harpoon-thrower. One action to load (Strength or a Boatswain), one to fire: +6 to hit, range 120 ft, 3d10 piercing. On a hit it roots the Saltwyrm (no Submerge or Ram) until it tears free — escape DC 16, or 25 damage cuts the line.

Crew actions (where seafaring specialties shine):

  • Helm / run for the reef (Helmsman). A check to steer for the coral. Each success advances the Reef Clock and halves the next Ram.
  • Repair the hull (Boatwright). A tools check restores 2d8 HP or seals a breach.
  • Cut the coil / secure the crew (Boatswain). Free a constricted ship faster, or lash an ally so they can't be dragged overboard.
  • Call the dive (Lookout). Grant allies advantage on the next attack, or warn the helm of a Ram (braced crew ignore prone/overboard).
  • Work the harpoon-thrower. Load or fire the line-ballista.

The Reef Clock. Track a 6-segment clock. Helm successes (and favorable rounds) fill it. When it fills, the ship reaches the coral, the Saltwyrm breaks off, and you cut to the reef landing — a guaranteed exit even if the dice turn cruel. A deadly solo set-piece is right for a party that wants a fight; the clock keeps it from becoming a wipe. If they'd rather kill it, let them — below half HP it may flee to deep water.

Getting the launch through the gap is a set-piece, not a cutscene. Run it as 4 group successes before 3 failures; each round, every PC takes one relevant action (DC 15): Athletics to row against the surge, Acrobatics to ride the launch over a coral shelf, Survival/Nature to call the safe line, Perception to spot submerged teeth, Sleight of Hand/Dex (tools) to patch a holed hull.

On a failed round, pick a cost:

  • The launch grinds coral: each PC makes a DC 13 Dex save or takes 2d6 slashing and is knocked into the water.
  • The undertow drags a PC toward the rocks: Strength save DC 14 or be swept 20 ft toward a coral spire (restrained until they break free).
  • Gear is lost overboard — a small, sharp loss the Pall will soon make hard to even remember owning.

Optional skirmish: if the Saltwyrm drew blood, 3 Hunter Sharks circle the gap. The shore ends it; they won't beach.

Adjusted-XP thresholds, roughly: easy 1,750 · medium 3,750 · hard 5,500 · deadly 8,500 (apply the ×1.5–×2 multiplier when several monsters share a fight).

Where Fight Weight
The approach Saltwyrm (reach-the-reef) Deadly, with an exit
The reef Coral challenge + optional 3 Hunter Sharks Hazard + hard
The Whisperwood 5–6 Mistlings in a fogwall Medium–hard
The Hunt 1 Pale Sentinel + 4 Mistlings Hard
The Pale Court 2 Pale Sentinels Hard
The Ballroom (optional) 6 Mistlings + 2 Hollows Hard
The Throne The Court's Sword (+2–3 Mistlings) Deadly boss

The vault holds wealth enough to satisfy any debt — if the party can carry it out without carrying its price.

  • Pale Coin of Velmaire (treasure, valuable but cursed). Old-world gold, minted by no living realm — worth a fortune to the right collector and worth nothing to anyone who can't be told where it came from without questions. While a PC carries a hoard of it, they make a Fading save after each long rest until it's spent or surrendered. The island's wealth wants to be remembered.
  • The Remembrance Cord (wondrous item, uncommon). A braided cord set with a single pale pearl, found around the neck of a portrait-subject. While worn, you have advantage on Fading saves, and once per long rest you can step your own Fading back by one. A genuine boon — and a sign that someone, once, was trying to remember too.
  • The Baron's Signet / the half-erased Journal (plot item). Not loot so much as the key: the surviving record of Aurel Velmaire's name and fall. Whoever pieces it together holds the "name it" ending in their hands.
  • One signature relic to taste — a Cloak of Mists (brief invisibility in fog), a Pearl of Power, or a pale-court blade — fitting a level 7 reward and the palace's foreign make.

To the player: You've seen Maurei on three old charts, drawn in three different places, by three different hands — each ringed with the same warning. An island that won't hold still on a map is impossible. Unless it's the oldest true thing left in the world, and everything else is what moved. You have to know which. GM truth: The charts disagree because Maurei genuinely shifts within the Pall — and because it is a fragment of the pre-drowning world, older than the ocean itself. This PC's instinct that "everything else moved" is correct, and brushes the setting's deepest secret. Reward their Investigation; let the Hall of Faces and the echoes pay them off.

To the player: Someone you love sailed for Maurei and never came back. A year later, the lone survivor of that voyage washed into your home port — aged forty years in one, hollow-eyed, speaking of a pale court and a white fox. They wouldn't say your loved one's name. Then they walked into the sea. You're going to bring them home, one way or another. GM truth: The lost loved one is Lost (Fading stage 5) — now an echo wandering Maurei, very possibly among the dancers in the Ballroom. They can be recovered, but only by a strong Anchor (this PC speaking a true memory, holding a shared object) and likely at the cost of completing the "name it" ending. A heartbreaking, earnable reunion. The survivor from this voyage is the source of Secret 5's journal — link the two players.

To the player: You owe the wrong person, and the clock is loud. But you've heard the same story in three ports now: a drowned palace on Maurei, stuffed with gold of a kind not minted anywhere in the Triangle. One good haul and the debt's gone. People say no one comes back from Maurei — but people say a lot of things to keep the treasure for themselves. GM truth: The gold is real (the Vault) and the Pale Coin is genuinely priceless — and genuinely cursed with the Pall's memory-price. This PC's greed is the engine that drives the party into the palace; their reckoning is whether to keep coin that won't let itself be forgotten. Great pressure for a "trade it" or "flee it" ending.

To the player: The signs have been wrong for a season. Hinaroa set in the wrong quarter. You've dreamed, three nights running, of a white fox that sits and asks you a question you wake before answering. Your tradition — or the small cult that found you — names a mercy-god of the mist who lives on Maurei and takes away grief by taking away its memory. You mean to find it, and serve it, or free it. You haven't decided which. GM truth: The "mercy-god" is Hauiri — a fallen power, and its "mercy" is dissolution. This PC is the table's natural bridge to interacting with the fox instead of fleeing it; their dreamed question is literally the fox's Ask. Whether the merciful read or the tragic read is true is theirs to discover — and to decide what worship of a forgetting-god should mean.

To the player: You hold a dead sailor's journal — bought, or stolen, or pressed into your hands as they died, with an oath you couldn't refuse: go back. The pages are water-ruined and, stranger, half-erased, as if the ink forgot itself. Names are missing. One survives, written hard enough to scar the page — a name that isn't an island's, isn't a god's, isn't anything you recognize. You're meant to do something with it. You're just not sure if it's to save someone, or to end something. GM truth: This is the survivor of Secret 2's expedition, and the journal is the key to Maurei. Its erased pages are Fading made literal; the one name that survived is Aurel Velmaire — assemble the full name (journal + portrait plate + throne inscription) and speak it at the throne that faces the world, and the "name it" ending opens. Link this PC tightly to Secret 2.

• Fading save: Wis save, DC 10 (Strand) / 13 (island & fox Presence) / 15 (palace), +1 per prior failure that scene. Fail → advance one stage. Five stages: Haze · Slip · Drift · Unmooring · Lost.
  • Anchors (step back one): a companion's true specific memory · a treasured object from home · a few minutes at a Stillpoint · answering the fox truthfully · leaving the island.
  • Stillpoints: the Pall thins where something is too remembered to forget. Rest = recovery; remnants within 20 ft have disadvantage (Anchored Ground).
  • The fox cannot be fought. Immune to all damage/conditions; disperses 1 round at most; never hostile. It can Lead, Take, Show, or Ask.
  • The name: three fragments — the wreck-journal (Secret 5), the Hall of Faces nameplate, the throne inscription. Spoken at the throne that faces the world = the release.
  • Endings: Name it (release + sinking island + race to the boat) · Trade it (bargain for the prize and passage) · Flee it (grab and run before anyone Fades past saving).
  • Pacing safety valve: when a table is drowning, give them a Stillpoint.
  • Convergence: Secrets 2 & 5 share an expedition; 1 & 3 pull toward the palace; 4 opens dialogue with the fox; 5 carries the name that unlocks the best ending.
  • Never on-screen in this one-shot: Arkhand by name, the Dreamer, the Drowned, or any statement of what the Divine Beasts are. The island only implies.

You have a heading and a name. Maurei. It sits on the charts like a smudge someone tried to rub out — drawn in three different places by three different hands, ringed each time with the same warning: coral, mist, do not land.

But each of you carries a reason to land anyway. A debt. A grave. A question. A map bought from a dying man. The captain who agreed to bring you this far will take you no closer than the reef-line — and even that, she says, is further than a sensible woman would go.

Ahead, the island is a low green hump wearing a crown of cloud that does not move with the wind. The sea around it has gone strangely quiet.

Built for roughly 3–4 hours of play: a hard approach and landing, a disorienting ascent, and a haunted palace where the island's secret — and the party's own memories — are the true stakes.

The Golden Triangle is a Polynesian-inspired world that is nearly all ocean. Its people sail between scattered islands; navigation, memory, and the sea are everything. The sun is Arohena, the moon is Hinaroa (a celestial clock and steering-mark — no mystical power), the stars are Fetuariki. Divine Beasts are vast, unkillable powers that reshape islands where they pass. The Shadow Isles are remote mysteries at the chart's edge.

GM-ONLY, never player-facing before the campaign reveals it: the world was once a continent, Arkhand. Its barons made a pact with the Dreamer of the deep; the world drowned, and the barons were reborn as the Divine Beasts. Nothing in this one-shot states this — the island only gestures at it through foreign architecture, unreadable script, and echoes.

The Pall erodes memory. The GM may ask for Wisdom saving throws as you move deeper into Maurei. Fading has five stages: Haze, Slip, Drift, Unmooring, and Lost.

You can recover by speaking a true, specific memory about a companion, focusing on a treasured object from home, resting at a Stillpoint, answering Hauiri truthfully, or leaving the island. Before play, name one treasured object your character brought and one memory another character knows about them.

mistEvents.name: Fogwall
mistEvents.text: A wall of mist rises and splits the party in two; each group can hear but not reach the other. It thins in 1d10 minutes, or at a Stillpoint.

mistEvents.name: Tidewrong
mistEvents.text: The way back has changed: the path, reef-gap, or shoreline is no longer where the party left it. Re-navigation is required at disadvantage.

mistEvents.name: Timeslip
mistEvents.text: 1d4 hours vanish between one step and the next, or day flips to night. An interrupted rest grants no benefit; rations are mysteriously gone.

mistEvents.name: Rememory
mistEvents.text: An echo of the old world plays around the party. Watching requires a Fading save, but reveals a true clue.

mistEvents.name: The Pale Door
mistEvents.text: A doorway or stair appears in a blank wall or cliff. It is a real shortcut, but leads deeper toward Hauiri.

mistEvents.name: Doubling
mistEvents.text: A character meets a mist-double, or a companion is briefly replaced by a silent echo.

mistEvents.name: Stillness
mistEvents.text: All mist withdraws for one minute. Hauiri is suddenly close enough to touch, then disappears when anyone reaches for it.

mistEvents.name: The Question
mistEvents.text: Hauiri arrives and Asks: who are you? Center the scene on one character.
